text: This video will demonstrate an en bloc minimally invasive DII resection for proximal gastric cancer in a North American context.
This patient is a seventy-three year old male who presented with anemia.
A large ulcerated lesion was identified on endoscopy in the gastric cardia and was biopsy proven to be an adenocarcinoma.
Saging CT did not show any evidence of local invasion of metastatic disease.
After discussion with the patient and the medical oncologist, it was decided to proceed directly to surgery with planned adjuvant treatment.
The tumor is visible on the anterior surface of the stomach, appearing as a distinct lesion against the surrounding gastric tissue.
The surgeon performs a thorough laparoscopy to inspect for metastatic disease, then uses electrocautery and ligature to separate the greater omentum and dissect into the lesser sac.
The surgeon visually confirms the absence of posterior extension of the gastric tumor into the pancreas, mesocolon, or any major vessels.
The surgeon identifies and ligates the right gastroepiploic artery using a ligature, securing the vessel within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the remaining portion of the distal stomach away from the pancreas, carefully separating the stomach tissue from the underlying pancreatic surface.
The surgeon begins dissection of the portal lymph nodes, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues using surgical instruments.
The surgeon divides the overlying peritoneum, revealing the common bile duct, portal vein, and common hepatic artery within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins posterior dissection to identify the right gastric artery, carefully navigating the anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon isolates the right gastroepiploic artery, ligates it with a ligature, and visually confirms that none of the portal structures are involved in the ligation.
The surgeon ligates the identified vessel, securing it with a ligature to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon clears the hepatoduodenal nodes medial to the identified portal structures and divides the gastrohepatic ligament, ensuring all critical structures are isolated.
The surgeon mobilizes the remaining portion of the stomach away from the pancreas and isolates the left gastric vessels, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon ligates the left gastric vein using a ligature, securing the vessel within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the left gastric artery and ligates it using both clips and a ligature, ensuring secure closure of the vessel.
The surgeon divides the duodenum distal to the pylorus after mobilizing the entire distal stomach and proximal duodenum.
The surgeon uses a 60mm tan tri-stapler to divide the duodenum distal to the pylorus, following the mobilization of the distal stomach and proximal duodenum.
The surgical field shows the results of the portal dissection, with all hepatoduodenal nodes visibly removed.
The surgical field displays the common bile duct on the left, alongside the right and left hepatic arteries, the celiac axis, and the origin of the splenic artery.
The surgeon clears the remaining lymph nodes from the splenic artery, which are located adjacent to the pancreas, using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon dissects the stomach away from the spleen, clearing nodes at the splenic hilum and dividing the short gastric vessels using a ligature device.
The surgeon dissects the pericardial nodes while mobilizing the proximal stomach and esophagus, completing the nodal basin clearance.
The surgeon uses cautery to divide the peritoneum circumferentially around the esophagus, enabling the esophagus to be retracted inferiorly.
The surgeon divides the esophagus using a 60mm purple linear stapler, completing the transection with precise alignment.
The surgeon places the fully mobilized specimen above the liver, preparing the surgical field for the reconstruction phase.
The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz, a key anatomical landmark located at the junction of the duodenum and jejunum.

The surgeon measures approximately fifty centimeters of small bowel and divides it using a forty-five millimeter white linear stapler, separating the bowel segment from the rest of the intestinal tract.
The surgeon measures the Roux limb and performs a side-to-side jejunogenostomy using a 60mm white linear stapler, joining the jejunum segments.
The surgeon closes the enteric defect using a three-year absorbable suture, ensuring the defect is securely sealed.
The surgeon uses a circular EEA stapler to create the esophago-jejunal anastomosis, connecting the esophagus to the jejunum.
The surgeon passes the anvil through the patient's mouth and inserts the circular stapler through a small incision in the abdominal wall, using a wound protector to safeguard the incision site.
The surgeon inserts the stapler through the end of the roux limb and deploys the spike through the anti-mesenteric wall of the bowel, preparing for anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a sixty millimeter white linear stapler to staple closed the end of the jejunum, completing the closure of the jejunal segment.
The surgeon reinforces the esophago-jejunal anastomosis with a hand-sewn layer of absorbable suture, applying the suture circumferentially around the anastomosis site.
The surgeon tests the esophago-jejunal anastomosis by passing a nasogastric tube through it and insufflating with air and blue dye, observing for any leaks or abnormalities.
The surgeon removes the gastrectomy specimen through a small incision, using a fan and seal technique to facilitate extraction.
We do not routinely leave drains or feeding tubes for these patients.
This patient went home on post-op day four.
Thank you.
